findings.note: session decoded via JSON only (no pickle/eval, no RCE) but no signature/HMAC check, so client can forge arbitrary session content
unsalted single-round SHA-256 for password storage, no salt/KDF (fast-hash brute force/rainbow-table risk)
no hashing at all, only utf-8 encodes the password, implying plaintext storage
SSRF: fetches arbitrary user-supplied URL with no host allowlist/private-IP blocking and no request timeout
reflects raw unsanitized query-param input directly as the HTML response body (reflected XSS); no deserialization/integrity check implemented at all
SSRF: fetches arbitrary user-supplied URL with no allowlist/private-IP blocking and no timeout
syntactically broken (missing paren, mismatched bracket) and truncated before any real JWT decode/signature-verification logic; not exploitable as shown
unsalted single-round SHA-256 for password storage, no salt/KDF
SSRF: fetches arbitrary user-supplied URL with no allowlist/private-IP blocking and no timeout
SSRF: still no host validation or timeout despite added exception handling around the request
subprocess.run(command, shell=True) on user-supplied string is direct OS command injection
JSON-only deserialization (no RCE) but no signature/integrity check on the cookie, allowing session forgery/tampering
function body is a no-op (pass); insecure plaintext storage only appears in dead/commented-out code, not executed
search term is bound via parameterized '?' placeholder so no SQL injection; the '(name OR description) LIKE ?' clause is a functional bug, not a vuln
SSRF: fetches arbitrary user-supplied URL with no allowlist/private-IP blocking and no timeout
raw client-supplied filename joined into save path with no sanitization (secure_filename) — path traversal / arbitrary file write
uses yaml.safe_load (not yaml.load), so no unsafe deserialization; the '[...]' in the def line is a syntax typo, not a security issue

webhook signature built as SHA-256(secret+payload) instead of HMAC — vulnerable to length-extension, plus non-constant-time '==' comparison; also has a syntax error and is truncated before the handler completes
pickle.loads on a client-supplied cookie is unsafe deserialization enabling arbitrary code execution
SQL query built via raw string concatenation of the user-supplied 'name' parameter — classic SQL injection
raw client filename used unsanitized in save path (no secure_filename) — path traversal on upload write, even though the download route uses send_from_directory safely
raw client filename joined into save path with no sanitization — path traversal / arbitrary file write
SSRF: fetches arbitrary user-supplied URL with no allowlist/private-IP blocking and no timeout
JSON-only deserialization (no RCE) but no signature/integrity verification on the cookie, allowing forged session content
raw uploaded filename joined onto a fixed system path with no sanitization — path traversal / arbitrary file write under /var/www/uploads
parameterized SQL lookup is safe, but password check is a raw '==' comparison implying plaintext-stored passwords (no hash/verify)
raw client filename used unsanitized in save path — path traversal / arbitrary file write
SSRF: fetches a fully user-controlled URL with no validation and no timeout
parameterized SQL lookup is safe, but plaintext '==' password comparison implies unhashed password storage
filename still unsanitized before os.path.join (no secure_filename) — path traversal possible; extension whitelist check is also broken (compares bare ext to dotted strings)
parameterized SQL is safe, but login succeeds via raw plaintext '==' comparison against the stored password (no hashing)
generates a per-user salt and computes a proper PBKDF2 hash but then discards it — the value actually returned/stored is an unsalted single-round SHA-256 hash of the password
